summary: Keep SC-019 scoped to the emitted JS secret runtime. The robust fix is to return opaque secret wrappers from `resolve` and fail inspect/log/stringify/coercion off the same decision metadata instead of trying to special-case each host surface separately.
ergonomics: The secret runtime logic is centralized enough in `src/Clasp/Emit/JavaScript.hs` that the behavior change stayed local.
`test/Main.hs` already had realistic end-to-end secret scenarios, so adding a real Node inspect/log regression was straightforward.
The quoted-string JS emitter remains brittle; small runtime API changes require editing long string blocks and exact JSON expectations.
follow_ups: Move the larger secret runtime helper block into a reusable JS template/module to reduce stringly-typed emitter edits.
Add standardized host-facing helpers for common disclosure sinks if future tasks need more than raw `reveal({ reason })` calls.
Expand secret misuse regressions to other host adapters if they start handling resolved secret values directly.
warnings: Testing only `JSON.stringify` is insufficient; Node logging goes through the inspect hook and needs its own regression.
Diagnostic boundary names come from the actual boundary passed into `resolve`, which may differ from the surface name you intuit from the test scenario.
Leaving any usable `.value` field on resolved secrets undermines the explicit reveal boundary, even if other coercion paths are blocked.
residual_risks: `reveal({ reason })` currently returns the raw host value once a reason is supplied; richer disclosure envelopes or audit sinks would need a follow-up change.
This task updates the emitted JavaScript runtime path only, so any future non-JS backend will need matching opaque-secret semantics.
